titleStmt.title: Patterns of Play: A Computational Approach to Understanding Game Mechanics

keywords.term: I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

div.head: Abstract
div.p: This study uses a computational approach to analyze game mechanics using a collection of 296 predefined mechanics. By identifying their occurrence in a large dataset of games, we reveal trends in their popularity, evolution over time, and their relationships to game genres, demonstrating the potential of the method for "computational game studies".

div.head: Introduction
div.p: Game studies have traditionally used critical and hermeneutic approaches to analyze the multifaceted nature of digital games, while digital humanities often rely on quantitative and computational methods to identify patterns in large collections of cultural artifacts, including games. Bridging these disciplines, Burghardt and Piontkowitz (2024) introduced the concept of computational game studies, proposing proxies and simplifications to facilitate the quantitative study of specific aspects within games, such as language, aesthetics and music. Extending this approach, we propose the consideration of game mechanics as an additional viable feature for large-scale computational analysis. Defined by Lundgren and Björk (2003) as “any part of the rule system of a game that covers one [...] possible kind of interaction that takes place during the game,” game mechanics provide a finite (yet extensible), systematically catalogued set of features, as illustrated in resources such as the Patterns in Game Design database (Björk / Holopainen, 2004). Previous research on mechanics has predominantly relied on qualitative methods, focusing on limited case studies (see for example Sicart, 2008; Zagal et. al., 2008; Braithwaite, 2018). While few examples of computational game studies have focused on genre classification (Jiangh / Zheng, 2023; Faisal / Peltoniemi, 2018) and board game mechanics (Samarasinghe et al., 2021), a data-driven approach to analyzing game mechanics in digital games remains unexplored. To address this gap, we introduce a novel methodology for extracting and analyzing game mechanics at scale from unstructured walkthroughs – textual descriptions of digital games (see Newman, 2011; Nylund, 2015). Understanding game mechanics offers new perspectives on the evolution of games in relation to industry, technology, and culture by identifying quantifiable features and their interactions with these phenomena, that go beyond metadata such as genre descriptions. Our initial experiments examining pattern distribution across genres, temporal pattern trends, and game similarity based on pattern usage demonstrate the potential of this methodology to facilitate novel research directions.
Figure 1: Detail view from the UMAP visualization of the pattern embeddings.

To analyze the in-game composition of game mechanics, we aggregate the sentence-level similarity vectors for each game. To represent a game based on its most salient patterns, we first sum pattern similarities above a 0.5 threshold across all sentences for a single game. We then normalize the resulting vector to ensure that its elements sum to 1, and keep only the top 15 normalized weights, setting the rest to 0.

div.head: Game Design Patterns in Selected Games
div.p: Next, we demonstrate that this method of mapping game design patterns allows games to be represented as vectors of patterns, facilitating various analytical applications. We chose two games from fundamentally different genres: Assassin's Creed, a franchise that blends historical fiction with action-adventure and stealth gameplay, and Civilization, a turn-based strategy game in which players lead a civilization through the ages by expanding their empire, researching technologies, and interacting with other civilizations to achieve victory. The assigned game mechanics are shown in Table 1.
Table 1: Game design patterns for the games Civilization and Assassin’s Creed. The weights are the normalized values from the summed pattern similarities.

Table 1 demonstrates that game patterns effectively distinguish between games, enabling large-scale comparisons that reveal relationships between mechanics, individual games, and genres. Table 2 shows the distribution of six patterns across genres. Analysis of the top 100 games per pattern reveals the prevalence of certain patterns within specific genres, such as fog of war in strategy games or aim & shoot in action games, suggesting that these patterns act as trigger mechanics that shape genre characteristics.
Table 2: Most popular genres by selected game patterns

div.head: Popularity of Mechanics
div.p: The corpus includes release year data for a subset of games, enabling analysis of the temporal distribution of game mechanics. This approach facilitates detailed investigations into the evolution and understanding of game characteristics, including trends in popularity. To analyze the prevalence of mechanics over time, we considered a mechanic present in a game if its associated value was greater than zero. We then aggregated the presence of each mechanic across all games released within the same year. Given the uneven temporal distribution within the corpus, we normalized counts by dividing by the total number of games per year. This provides a normalized measure for the popularity and distribution of game mechanics, as illustrated by the patterns of enemies and score in Figure 2.
Figure 2: Time series analysis of the enemies pattern remains relatively stable, while the score pattern trend exhibits a decline over time.

While enemies maintains stable prevalence, score declines over time, indicating a shift away from traditional scoring systems. However, employing heatmaps with clustered correlated time series reveal more nuanced trends across numerous mechanics, uncovering practices of game pattern usage within the industry. This approach provides deeper insight into the evolution and interplay of mechanics, as shown in Figure 3.
Figure 3: Heatmap visualizing the temporal prevalence of multiple game design patterns. Color intensity reflects pattern popularity.

Our analysis reveals distinct trends in the evolution of game mechanics. Persistent mechanics such as spawn points and levels maintain high prevalence, with spawn points gradually increasing, suggesting later integration into game design. Conversely, save points, score, and power-ups decline, indicating a shift away from traditional elements. The rise of rewards in the 2000s and 2010s reflects a growing emphasis on player engagement through intricate reward systems and character progression. These observations highlight the dynamic evolution of game mechanics, driven by changing design philosophies, technological advances, and player expectations.

div.head: Game Similarity and Clustering
div.p: In addition, our approach allows for similarity comparisons, which is useful for understanding genre boundaries, identifying similar games, and developing novel search functions for game libraries. Using UMAP for dimensionality reduction and DBSCAN clustering (Pedregosa et al., 2011), we show that genre information from the corpus is not consistent with similarities based on game patterns (see Figure 5). This discrepancy highlights a research opportunity to investigate the relationship between game pattern similarities and established genre concepts. Figure 4 demonstrates that pre-selected games with similar functionalities and themes have a small cosine distance, indicating a high degree of similarity.

Figure 4: A distance matrix of pre-selected games reveals that titles with similar genres or gameplay patterns exhibit low distances, indicating that pat-tern vectors capture properties reflecting comparable player experiences.

Figure 5: UMAP visualization (5a) colored by genre shows diverse genre distribution across game pattern similarity structures. DBscan clustering (5b) reveals that pattern similarity transcends genre classification.

div.head: Constructing Game Mechanics: Pattern Cooccurrences
div.p: Analyzing game pattern connections reveals broader mechanical contexts within games. Using game pattern vectors from our corpus, we identify statistically significant co-occurrences of all game patterns and visualize them using force-directed layout graphs, with colors representing potential communities. Graph analysis reveals clusters of highly interconnected patterns, suggesting distinct mechanical systems (see Figure 6). The presence of multiple connected components suggests that our method can reveal broader relationships between game mechanics and design principles. For example, clusters related to resources and information highlight the potential for targeted analysis of specific game design choices and their underlying mechanics. The revealed structure broadly aligns with the chapter organization in Lundgren & Björk’s (2003) book, validating their pattern categorization with our extensive dataset.
Figure 6: Graph analysis of game pattern connectivity, with magnified highly connected cliques (modularity).

div.head: Discussion and Conclusion
div.p: While our approach is already promising, it has some limitations. The precision of mechanic identification could be improved, as the current method may be sensitive to noise in the walkthrough texts. In addition, relying on walkthroughs may introduce a bias toward mechanics that are explicitly discussed in these guides. Moreover, we only used the published game design patterns from the original CSV release, as we needed to first verify the legitimacy of the updated but unpublished patterns to maintain research credibility. Future work could explore alternative data sources, such as game design documents or player-generated content, to improve the accuracy and completeness of the extraction of game mechanics.

Despite these limitations, our method offers a foundation for future research in computational game studies. Discrepancies between pattern similarity and genre classification call for further investigation into the complex relationship between mechanics and genre. Additionally, our approach allows for scaling existing analyses of mechanics, such as those by Zagal et al. (2008) on gameplay segmentation and Braithwaite (2018) on mechanic evolution, to larger datasets. The examination of game mechanics facilitates comprehensive investigations into their evolution, interactions with various genres and player dispositions, as well as their influence on design and user experience. This approach uncovers profound, quantifiable characteristics molded by industry trends, technological advancements, and design innovations. It transcends traditional genre classifications, offering enhanced insights into the details of game complexity, design frameworks, and the expansive cultural and economic landscapes that shape the ludological progression of digital games.

listBibl.head: Bibliography
listBibl.bibl: Björk, Staffan / Jussi Holopainen. 2004. Patterns in Game Design. Game Development Series. Boston, Mass: Charles River Media.
Burghardt, Manuel / Vera Piontkowitz. 2024. “Computational Game Studies? Drei Annäherungsperspektiven.” In DHd 2024: Konferenzabstracts. Passau. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.10698402.
Braithwaite, Andrea. 2018. “WoWing Alone: The Evolution of ‘Multiplayer’ in World of Warcraft.” Games and Culture13 (2): 119–35. https://doi.org/10.1177/1555412015610246.

Clearwater, David. 2011. “What Defines Video Game Genre? Thinking about Genre Study after the Great Divide.” Loading... 5 (8).
https://journals.sfu.ca/loading/index.php/loading/article/view/67.

Faisal, Ali / Mirva Peltoniemi. 2018. “Establishing Video Game Genres Using Data-Driven Modeling and Product Databases.” Games and Culture 13 (1): 20–43.
https://doi.org/10.1177/1555412015601541 .

Jiang, Yuhang / Lukun Zheng. 2023. “Deep Learning for Video Game Genre Classification.” Multimedia Tools and Applications 82 (14): 21085–99.
https://doi.org/10.1007/s11042-023-14560-5 .

Lundgren, Sus / Staffan Björk. 2003. “Game Mechanics: Describing Computer-Augmented Games in Terms of Interaction.” In Proceedings of the 1st International Conference on Technologies for Interactive Digital Storytelling and Entertainment. Darmstadt, Germany.

Liebl, Bernhard / Burghardt, Manuel. 2020. “Shakespeare in the Vectorian Age” – An evaluation of different word embeddings and NLP parameters for the detection of Shakespeare quotes. In Proceedings of the The 4th Joint SIGHUM Workshop on Computational Linguistics for Cultural Heritage, Social Sciences, Humanities and Literature: 58–68. International Committee on Computational Linguistics. https://aclanthology.org/2020.latechclfl-1.7/

McInnes, Leland / John Healy / James Melville. 2018. “UMAP: Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection for Dimension Reduction.” arXiv.
https://doi.org/10.48550/ARXIV.1802.03426 .

Newman, James. 2011. “(Not) Playing Games: Player-Produced Walkthroughs as Archival Documents of Digital Gameplay.” International Journal of Digital Curation 6 (2): 109–27.
https://doi.org/10.2218/ijdc.v6i2.206 .

Nylund, Niklas. 2015. “Walkthrough and Let’s Play: Evaluating Preservation Methods for Digital Games.” In Proceedings of the 19th International Academic Mindtrek Conference, 55–62. Tampere Finland: ACM.
https://doi.org/10.1145/2818187.2818283 .

Pedregosa, Fabian / Gaël Varoquaux / Alexandre Gramfort / Vincent Michel / Bertrand Thirion / Olivier Grisel / Mathieu Blondel / et al. 2011. “Scikit-Learn: Machine Learning in Python.” Journal of Machine Learning Research 12 (85): 2825–30.

Pellicer, Lucas F. A. O. / Taynan Maier Ferreira / Anna Helena Reali Costa. 2023. “Data Augmentation Techniques in Natural Language Processing.” Applied Soft Computing 132 (January):109803.
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.asoc.2022.109803 .

Reimers, Nils / Iryna Gurevych. 2019. “Sentence-BERT: Sentence Embeddings Using Siamese BERT-Networks.” In Proceedings of the 2019 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing and the 9th International Joint Conference on Natural Language Processing (EMNLP-IJCNLP), 3980–90. Hong Kong, China: Association for Computational Linguistics.
https://doi.org/10.18653/v1/D19-1410 .

Samarasinghe, Dilini / Michael Barlow / Erandi Lakshika / Timothy Lynar / Nour Moustafa / Thomas Townsend / Benjamin Turnbull. 2021. “A Data Driven Review of Board Game Design and Interactions of Their Mechanics.” IEEE Access 9:114051–69.
https://doi.org/10.1109/ACCESS.2021.3103198 .

Sicart, Miguel. 2008. “Defining Game Mechanics.” Game Studies 8 (2).
https://gamestudies.org/0802/articles/sicart .
Tiepmar, Jochen / Manuel Burghardt. 2021. “Game Walkthrough Corpus (GWTC).” Zenodo. https://doi.org/10.5281/ZENODO.4562336.

Zagal, José P. / Clara Fernández-Vara / Michael Mateas. 2008. “Rounds, Levels, and Waves: The Early Evolution of Gameplay Segmentation.” Games and Culture 3 (2): 175–98.
https://doi.org/10.1177/1555412008314129 .
